content.text: Project Knowledge is a feature of Claude.ai projects that lets you upload documents, code files, or text snippets as persistent reference material. These files are included in the context of every conversation in the project, giving Claude access to domain-specific information, reference materials, and background context without any manual re-uploading.
What Project Knowledge Is

In a Claude.ai project, Knowledge (sometimes labeled 'Files' or 'Knowledge base' depending on the interface version) is a collection of files and text snippets that Claude can reference in every conversation within the project. Unlike files uploaded to individual messages — which are only visible in that one conversation — project knowledge files are persistent: they are automatically loaded as context for every new conversation in the project.

Project knowledge occupies part of Claude's context window in every conversation. This means Claude has the content of your knowledge files available for reference when generating responses, even without you mentioning them explicitly in the conversation.

attrs.alt: Project Knowledge: persistent reference files for all project conversations

content.text: Why Project Knowledge Matters

The most valuable knowledge for an AI assistant is specific to you — your company's style guide, your codebase's architecture, your product's feature set, your research notes, your client's background. This knowledge is not in Claude's training data, and describing it from scratch in every conversation is tedious and error-prone.

Project knowledge solves this by making your specific context available automatically. A support team that uploads its product FAQ as project knowledge gets Claude answers that reflect actual product behavior, not Claude's general guess about what the product does. A developer who uploads an API specification gets code generation that matches the real API rather than an invented one. A writer who uploads a style guide gets editing suggestions that match the specific style, not generic 'good writing' advice.

attrs.alt: How knowledge files integrate with instructions and messages

content.text: What Can Be Uploaded as Project Knowledge
Documents
PDF
— product documentation, research papers, regulatory texts, reports
DOCX/DOC
— Word documents: briefs, specifications, meeting minutes
TXT
— plain text files: notes, logs, transcripts
Markdown (.md)
— documentation written in Markdown, README files, wikis
Data and Configuration
CSV
— lookup tables, reference data, structured lists
JSON
— configuration files, API schemas, structured reference data
YAML
— configuration files, OpenAPI specs
XML
— configuration or data in XML format
Code Files

Uploading code files as knowledge is useful when you want Claude to understand your existing codebase patterns, conventions, or a specific module. This is particularly valuable for code generation or review projects where you want Claude's output to match your existing code style and architecture.
Text Snippets

Some interfaces allow you to add text directly as a knowledge snippet, without uploading a file. This is convenient for short reference text — a list of terms and definitions, a set of rules, a short template — that does not exist as a separate file on your machine.
Uploading Files to Project Knowledge
Open the project in Claude.ai and navigate to the Knowledge or Files section (usually a tab or sidebar section in the project view).
Click the Add files button or drag files onto the upload area.
Select one or more files from your device.
Wait for the upload to complete — files are processed by Claude.ai's backend to extract text content.
Optionally rename the file or add a description to clarify its contents.
The file now appears in the project's knowledge list and will be included in all future conversations.

Note: uploading a new file or updating an existing file affects only new conversations and new messages in currently open conversations. Messages sent before the upload did not have access to the new file.
Size and Count Limits
Project knowledge has limits on file size and total knowledge base size. These limits exist because knowledge files occupy context window space in every conversation:
Limit
Typical Value
Notes
Max file size
~30 MB
Larger files may be truncated or rejected
Total knowledge size
Varies by plan
Affects how much context is consumed
Number of files
Varies
More files = more context consumed
File types
Text-extractable
Scanned-only PDFs may not be readable

Context window consumption is the key constraint in practice. Even if uploading a 10 MB PDF is allowed, that PDF's extracted text might be very long and consume a large portion of Claude's context window, leaving less room for the actual conversation. This can cause Claude to lose track of earlier messages in a long conversation.
How Claude Uses Project Knowledge
Automatic Inclusion

Claude does not retrieve knowledge on demand like a database — it does not run a semantic search query to find the relevant parts of your knowledge files. Instead, the text content of all knowledge files is included in Claude's context at the start of every conversation. Claude reads all of it in every conversation, just as it reads the instructions and the conversation history.

This means the quality of Claude's knowledge-informed responses depends on whether the knowledge files actually contain the relevant content and whether that content is clearly written and well-structured. Poorly organized knowledge files produce less consistent knowledge use.
Referencing Knowledge in Conversations

Users can explicitly ask Claude to refer to project knowledge files: 'Based on the style guide in the project knowledge, how should I format this section?' or 'Check the API spec file and tell me what parameters the /users endpoint accepts.'

Claude can also draw on knowledge implicitly, without being asked — if you upload a coding standards guide and then ask Claude to write a function, it should apply the conventions from that guide automatically. Whether this happens reliably depends on how explicit and well-organized the knowledge file is.
Best File Formats for Project Knowledge
Not all file formats are equally useful as project knowledge. The best formats are those that produce clean, structured text when parsed:
Format
Quality as Knowledge
Notes
Plain text (.txt)
Excellent
Direct, no parsing overhead
Markdown (.md)
Excellent
Structure preserved
CSV
Good
Tabular data reads well
PDF (searchable)
Good
Text extraction generally works
DOCX
Good
Text and basic structure extracted
PDF (scanned)
Poor
No text to extract without OCR
XLSX (complex)
Fair
Multiple sheets and formulas may not parse cleanly
Practical Use Cases
Style Guide as Knowledge

Upload your organization's or publication's style guide as a knowledge file. Claude will apply the specific rules — preferred vocabulary, formatting conventions, tone guidance — consistently across all conversations in the writing project, without needing to be reminded each time.
API Specification as Knowledge

For development projects, upload the OpenAPI/Swagger specification for the API being built or consumed. When generating API-calling code, Claude will produce calls with the correct endpoint paths, method names, and parameter shapes from the spec rather than guessing.

# Example: asking Claude to use the spec from knowledge
"Using the API spec in the project knowledge, write a Python function
that calls the POST /auth/login endpoint with email and password parameters
and handles the 401 error response."

# Claude will check the spec for:
# - Correct endpoint path
# - Required request body fields
# - Response schema
# - Authentication header format
Product Documentation as Knowledge

For support or documentation projects, upload the current product documentation or user manual. Claude can then answer questions about the product with reference to actual documented behavior rather than hallucinating capabilities.
Research Notes as Knowledge

For research projects, upload literature notes, interview transcripts, or preliminary findings. Claude can synthesize across these materials when asked to generate insights, summaries, or analysis — treating your uploaded notes as the source of truth for project-specific claims.
Glossary and Terminology

Domain-specific terminology is a common source of AI confusion. Upload a glossary of key terms, acronyms, and their definitions as project knowledge. Claude will then use these terms consistently and correctly throughout the project.
Managing Knowledge Files
Keeping Files Updated

Knowledge files become stale as the underlying source material changes. An API spec uploaded six months ago may describe deprecated endpoints. A product FAQ from a year ago may be outdated after several feature releases. Establish a cadence for reviewing and updating project knowledge files — monthly at minimum for fast-moving projects.
Organizing Multiple Files

When a project has many knowledge files, give each a clear, descriptive name. If the interface supports descriptions, add a one-sentence description explaining what each file contains and when it was last updated. This helps you maintain the knowledge base over time and helps Claude identify which file to reference for specific questions.
Removing Outdated Files

Delete outdated knowledge files promptly. An outdated spec is worse than no spec — Claude may confidently cite superseded information from it. When you upload an updated version of a file, delete the old version to avoid conflicts.
Limitations of Project Knowledge
Not a Database

Project knowledge is not a queryable database. Claude does not index knowledge files with a vector store and perform similarity search to retrieve the most relevant chunks. It reads the entire knowledge content as part of the context. This works well for small, well-organized knowledge bases but does not scale to very large document collections.
Context Window Competition

Knowledge files compete with conversations for context window space. If total knowledge file content is very large, Claude may not be able to hold a long conversation without losing track of early messages — because the context window fills up with knowledge content and recent messages, and older messages must drop off. Keep knowledge files as concise as possible: remove redundant sections, condense verbose prose, and split large files into project-specific subsets.
No Real-Time Updates

Knowledge files represent a snapshot of content at the time of upload. Changes to the underlying source (a product documentation update, a new version of an API spec) do not automatically update project knowledge — you must manually re-upload. For dynamically changing content, project knowledge is not the right mechanism; MCP servers connected to live data sources may be more appropriate.
Best Practices
Keep files concise and focused
— include only the content relevant to the project; omit lengthy boilerplate or sections Claude doesn't need
Use searchable, text-rich formats
— prefer plain text, Markdown, or searchable PDFs over scanned documents or complex spreadsheets
Name files descriptively
— 'style-guide-v3.md' is better than 'document1.pdf'
Update knowledge files regularly
— outdated context is misleading; establish a review cadence
Delete superseded files
— remove old versions when uploading updates to prevent conflicting information
Test after adding knowledge
— run test questions in a new conversation to verify Claude is using the knowledge correctly
Cross-reference in instructions
— if a knowledge file is particularly important, reference it by name in the project instructions: 'Refer to the style-guide.md in the project knowledge when editing'
Advanced Knowledge File Strategies
Chunking Large Documents

When a reference document is very large — a 200-page product manual, a complete API specification for a complex platform — uploading the entire document as a single knowledge file may fill too much of the context window to be practical. A better strategy is to split the document into topic-specific chunks and upload each as a separate file.

For example, a large API specification might be split into authentication.yaml, endpoints-users.yaml, endpoints-products.yaml, and webhooks.yaml. Each project conversation then has access to all chunks, but because each chunk is focused, the content is denser with relevant information relative to its size than the full document would be.
Summary Files for Long Documents

Another approach for long source documents is to create a summary knowledge file alongside the full document. The summary — a one-to-two-page distillation of the most important information — is much smaller and ensures the key points are prominently available in every context. Claude can refer to the summary for quick answers and the full document for detailed lookup.

# Example: Summary file format for an API specification

API Summary — Acme Platform API v2.3 (full spec: api-spec-v2.3.yaml)

BASE URL: https://api.acme.com/v2

AUTH: Bearer token in Authorization header. Tokens via POST /auth/token.
Token lifetime: 1 hour. Refresh via POST /auth/refresh.

KEY ENDPOINTS:
GET  /users          — list users (paginated, max 100/page)
POST /users          — create user (returns 201 with user object)
GET  /users/{id}     — get user by ID
PUT  /users/{id}     — full update
PATCH /users/{id}    — partial update

PAGINATION: cursor-based. Use next_cursor from response in ?cursor= param.

ERROR FORMAT: { error: string, code: string, details: object }
Common codes: INVALID_TOKEN, NOT_FOUND, RATE_LIMITED, VALIDATION_ERROR

RATE LIMITS: 100 req/min per token. 429 response when exceeded.
Structured Reference Tables

Tabular data stored as CSV or Markdown tables is among the most useful knowledge file formats. Tables of error codes, configuration options, feature flags, permission levels, or product SKUs give Claude structured reference data that it can apply precisely when answering questions about specific values.

# Example: feature-flags.csv knowledge file
feature_flag,enabled_plans,description
advanced_search,pro;team;enterprise,Full-text search with filters
custom_domains,team;enterprise,Map custom domain to workspace
api_access,pro;team;enterprise,API key generation and management
sso_saml,enterprise,SAML 2.0 SSO integration
audit_logs,team;enterprise,90-day audit log retention
data_export,pro;team;enterprise,Export data in CSV or JSON
white_labeling,enterprise,Remove Acme branding
Knowledge Files for Different Project Types
Support and Customer-Facing Projects

Support projects benefit from knowledge files that Claude can directly cite when answering questions: an FAQ document, a known issues list with workarounds, an escalation guide, and a glossary of product terminology. When a customer describes an issue, Claude can check the known issues list and either provide the documented workaround or identify that the issue requires escalation.
FAQ document covering the 30–50 most common support questions with authoritative answers
Known issues list with current status (open, investigating, fixed in version X) and workarounds
Product feature matrix showing which features are available on which plans
Escalation criteria and routing guide for specific issue types
Customer communication templates for common scenarios (refund request, account suspension, data export)
Development and Engineering Projects

Engineering projects benefit from knowledge files that give Claude accurate, project-specific context that is not available in its training data: your codebase's architecture, your team's conventions, your tech stack versions, and your testing requirements. With these files available, code generation and review tasks produce output that fits your actual system rather than a generic equivalent.
Architecture overview document describing major components and their interactions
Coding standards and conventions (naming, structure, commenting requirements)
Database schema definition (SQL DDL or entity diagram description)
API specification files (OpenAPI/Swagger format)
Testing strategy document (test types required, coverage expectations, testing libraries)
Dependency list with versions (requirements.txt, package.json, go.mod)
Research and Analysis Projects

Research projects benefit from knowledge files that establish the factual foundation the project works within: background research documents, key literature summaries, data source inventories, and methodology documents. These files let Claude reason within a specific research context rather than having to rediscover the research foundation in every conversation.
Keeping Knowledge Files Synchronized With Source Documents

The most common failure mode for project knowledge is knowledge drift — the knowledge files become outdated while the underlying source documents are updated. Over months of active use, a project can accumulate knowledge files that describe an old version of a product, an expired policy, or a superseded technical design.
Version Control for Knowledge Files

Store knowledge files in the same version control system as the primary source documents whenever possible. This ensures that knowledge file updates happen as part of the same workflow as source document updates, rather than as an afterthought. A CI step that alerts when a key source document changes but the corresponding knowledge file has not been updated can catch drift proactively.
Naming Conventions for Versioning
Include version or date information in knowledge file names so that stakeholders can quickly identify the currency of the information:
Date-based naming
— 'api-spec-2025-07.yaml' makes the snapshot date explicit
Version-based naming
— 'style-guide-v3.md' ties the file to a specific document version
Source-and-version combined
— 'product-docs-v4.2-2025-06.pdf' is unambiguous
When you upload a new version of a knowledge file, always delete the old version at the same time. Leaving both versions creates conflicting information that will confuse Claude's responses.
Debugging Knowledge File Issues
Testing Whether Claude Is Reading the File

If you are unsure whether Claude is accessing a specific knowledge file, ask a question that can only be answered by its contents. For example, if you uploaded a glossary, ask for the definition of a term that only appears in that glossary. If Claude gives the correct definition, the file is accessible. If Claude gives a generic or wrong answer, the file may not be loaded or may not have been processed correctly.
Diagnosing Processing Failures

Some file types fail to process cleanly. Signs of a processing failure include Claude claiming it doesn't have access to the file, or giving answers that seem to ignore the file's contents entirely. To diagnose, check whether the file uploaded successfully (look for a confirmation indicator in the knowledge base UI), try re-uploading, and if the problem persists, convert the file to a simpler format (such as converting DOCX to plain text TXT).
Context Window Saturation

If project conversations seem to lose track of earlier messages in longer sessions, context window saturation may be the cause. The knowledge files plus instructions are consuming so much of the context window that the conversation history is truncated. To address this: reduce the total size of knowledge files by removing redundant content, split large files into smaller focused chunks, or accept that very long conversations in knowledge-heavy projects will have a shorter effective memory window.
Key Takeaways
Project Knowledge is a set of persistent files and text snippets available as context in every conversation within a Claude.ai project, without needing to be re-uploaded each session.
Best file formats are plain text, Markdown, searchable PDFs, and CSV — formats that produce clean extracted text without parsing complications.

Knowledge files are loaded in full into every conversation's context, not retrieved on demand; this works well for concise, focused reference material but does not scale to very large document collections.
Common use cases include style guides, API specifications, product documentation, research notes, and domain glossaries.
Keep knowledge files updated — stale files produce confidently wrong answers. Delete superseded versions when uploading replacements.
Knowledge files consume context window space; very large knowledge bases can crowd out conversation history in long chats.
Cross-reference important knowledge files in the project instructions to ensure Claude uses them reliably.
How to Use This Feature Effectively

Getting the most out of Project Knowledge & Files requires understanding both its capabilities and its limitations. This section provides concrete guidance on using the feature to its full potential, with practical examples you can apply immediately.

The key to effective use of Project Knowledge & Files is to start with clear intentions. Know what outcome you're aiming for before you begin, and set up the feature in a way that supports that outcome. Many users find it helpful to experiment with different approaches to discover what works best for their specific workflow.
Define your goal clearly before you start — vague intentions lead to vague results
Use the feature consistently to build familiarity and identify patterns
Combine with other Claude features for maximum effectiveness
Review outputs critically and provide feedback through follow-up prompts
Document what works for your use case so you can replicate success
Share knowledge with teammates to build organizational competency
Use Cases and Applications

There are many valuable use cases for Project Knowledge & Files across different contexts. Understanding the range of applications helps you identify where this feature can add the most value to your workflow.
Professional productivity: Streamline repetitive tasks and focus on higher-value work
Research and analysis: Gather and synthesize information more efficiently
Content creation: Generate first drafts, outlines, and creative ideas
Learning and education: Explore new topics and get explanations of complex concepts
Problem-solving: Work through challenges with an AI thinking partner
Code assistance: Write, review, debug, and explain code
Communication: Draft, edit, and polish written communications
Integration with Your Workflow
Integrating Project Knowledge & Files into your existing workflow is usually straightforward, but there are several strategies that can make the transition smoother and ensure you get maximum value.

Start by identifying the specific tasks where you'll use this feature most. Then create a small experiment to test how it fits into your existing process. Adjust your workflow based on what you learn, and gradually expand usage as you build confidence.
Map out your current workflow before adding Claude to identify integration points
Start with one specific task to build confidence before expanding usage
Create templates or prompts that you can reuse across similar tasks
Set up a feedback loop to continuously improve your approach
Track time savings and quality improvements to build the business case
Configuration and Settings

Configuring Project Knowledge & Files correctly for your needs is important for getting consistent, high-quality results. While the defaults work well for most use cases, understanding the available options allows you to fine-tune the behavior.

Take time to explore the settings and options available. Each setting serves a specific purpose, and understanding what each one does gives you greater control over the output. Don't be afraid to experiment — you can always revert to defaults if needed.
Review all available settings before assuming defaults are optimal for your use case
Make changes incrementally so you can understand the impact of each adjustment
Save your preferred configurations for easy reuse across sessions
Test configuration changes with representative examples before relying on them in production
Document your configuration choices and the reasons behind them
Tips and Tricks

Experienced users of Project Knowledge & Files have discovered many tips and tricks that significantly improve their results. These insights often come from experimentation and aren't always obvious from the documentation.
Use specific, concrete language rather than vague requests for better results
Break complex tasks into smaller steps for more reliable outcomes
Provide context and examples when working on specialized or domain-specific tasks
Use follow-up prompts to refine and improve initial outputs
Combine this feature with other Claude capabilities for compound benefits
Learn from both successes and failures to continuously improve your approach
Share tips with your team to build collective expertise
Detailed Walkthrough

This walkthrough takes you through Project Knowledge & Files step by step, explaining not just what to do but why each step matters. Following along will build both your practical skills and your conceptual understanding.

One of the most common challenges people face with Project Knowledge & Files is understanding how all the pieces fit together. This walkthrough addresses that by building up from first principles, showing how each component contributes to the overall result.
Step 1: Understand the goal — what specific outcome are you trying to achieve?
Step 2: Gather prerequisites — ensure you have everything needed before starting
Step 3: Configure the basics — set up the minimal working configuration first
Step 4: Test the basics — verify the simple case works before adding complexity
Step 5: Add features incrementally — build up functionality one piece at a time
Step 6: Handle edge cases — ensure robust behavior for unusual inputs
Step 7: Optimize and polish — refine the implementation for production use
Real-World Scenarios

Understanding how Project Knowledge & Files applies to real-world scenarios helps bridge the gap between learning and application. These scenarios represent common situations you'll encounter in practice.

Scenario 1: A startup team wants to quickly build a proof-of-concept. They need to move fast, so they prioritize simplicity and speed over optimization. This is the perfect situation to use the minimal configuration and iterate.

Scenario 2: An enterprise with strict compliance requirements needs to implement this feature with full audit logging, access controls, and documentation. They'll spend more time upfront designing the architecture correctly.
Scenario 3: A solo developer building a personal project wants maximum flexibility and customization. They can afford to experiment more freely because the stakes are lower.
Match your approach to your context — startups and enterprises have different constraints
Document your decisions early, even in prototypes, to avoid losing context later
Build in testing and validation from the start, regardless of the project size
Consider future requirements, but don't over-engineer for hypothetical needs
Troubleshooting Common Issues
When working with Project Knowledge & Files, you'll likely encounter some common issues. Understanding these in advance saves significant time and frustration.
Issue 1 — Getting unexpected results: This is often caused by incomplete or ambiguous instructions. Review your input carefully, be more specific about what you want, and try providing examples.

Issue 2 — Performance is slower than expected: Check if you're using the right model for your use case, consider whether you can reduce the amount of context you're sending, and look for opportunities to cache results.
Issue 3 — Inconsistent outputs: Add more constraints and examples to your prompts, use structured output formats, and consider breaking complex tasks into simpler subtasks.
Log all inputs and outputs during development to diagnose issues
Compare against known-good examples when outputs seem wrong
Check the Anthropic documentation for recent changes that might affect behavior
Use the Claude.ai interface to test prompts before integrating via the API
Advanced Configuration
Once you've mastered the basics of Project Knowledge & Files, you can explore advanced configuration options that unlock additional capabilities.
Custom parameters: Adjust model parameters to fine-tune behavior for your specific use case
System prompts: Use system-level instructions to establish consistent behavior across interactions
Multi-turn conversations: Leverage conversation history to build context over multiple turns
Tool integration: Combine with Claude's tool use capabilities for more powerful applications
Caching strategies: Use prompt caching to reduce costs and latency for repeated requests
Streaming: Implement streaming responses for real-time user experiences
Batch processing: Process multiple requests efficiently using batch APIs
Summary

This section provided a comprehensive exploration of Project Knowledge & Files, from basic usage through advanced scenarios. The key takeaways are: start simple and iterate, match your approach to your context, and always test with representative examples. With these foundations in place, you're ready to apply these skills confidently in your own projects.
